title: SEGMENT 7 — The suicide program cancer has disabled

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — The suicide program cancer has disabled
I mentioned earlier that your cells are programmed to commit suicide when something goes wrong.
I want to take that seriously for a moment, because it sounds dramatic, and the actual biology is even more striking than the phrase suggests.
The program is called apoptosis — Greek for "falling off," like leaves falling from a tree, which is a beautiful name for what's really an orchestrated cellular self-dismantling.
Here's what apoptosis looks like at the cellular level.
The cell shrinks.
Its DNA gets chopped into neat regular fragments.
The cell membrane stays intact but starts flagging itself with "eat me" signals.
Then it breaks up into tidy little blebs called apoptotic bodies, which neighboring cells and immune cells engulf and recycle.
No mess.
No inflammation.
The cell quietly removes itself.
This is happening in you right now.
Billions of cells die by apoptosis in your body every day.
It's how your immune system prunes self-reactive T cells during development.
It's how your fingers form — you started in the womb with webbed hands, and the cells in between your fingers committed apoptosis on cue, leaving distinct digits.
It's how your gut lining renews itself.
Apoptosis is not failure; it's how the body grooms itself.
Now imagine you've just become a budding cancer cell.
You've acquired some weird mutations.
Your growth signals are abnormal.
Your DNA is damaged.
Under normal conditions, all of this would trigger apoptosis — the cell would sense the trouble and dismantle itself before it could become a problem.
Apoptosis is one of the body's main lines of defense against cancer.
Most pre-cancerous cells, in fact, do die this way.
You make pre-cancerous cells all the time, and they almost always get cleared.
For a cell to actually become cancer, it has to disable the suicide program.
Cancer is partly a story of cells that learned how to not die when they were supposed to die.
The machinery of apoptosis is a kind of internal court — there are accusers, defenders, and executioners.
The executioners are a family of proteins called caspases, which when activated, chop up the cell's internal structures.
The decision to release the caspases is made at the mitochondrial membrane, by a tense standoff between two opposing protein families.
On one side are the pro-survival proteins — BCL2 is the most famous.
They sit in the mitochondrial membrane and hold the death machinery in check, like prison guards keeping the executioners locked up.

On the other side are the pro-death proteins — BAX and BAK are the executioners themselves, and a third group called BH3-only proteins act as the sensors that detect cellular stress and call for execution.

When stress signals come in — DNA damage, oxidative stress, growth factor withdrawal — the BH3-only sensors get activated, they neutralize the BCL2 guards, the BAX and BAK executioners get released, they punch holes in the mitochondrial membrane, the contents pour into the cytoplasm, and the caspases fire.
Cell dies.
Cancer cells tilt this balance toward survival.

The classic example is a lymphoma called follicular lymphoma, where a chromosomal translocation puts the BCL2 gene next to a strong B-cell regulatory element, causing massive overproduction of the BCL2 pro-survival protein.
The prison is overstaffed with guards.
The executioners can never get out.
Cells that should die just keep living, accumulating into a slow-growing but ultimately lethal lymphoma.
The clever therapeutic response to this is one of my favorite drugs in modern oncology: venetoclax.
It's a small molecule that mimics the natural BH3-only sensor proteins — it slips into the binding pocket on BCL2 and neutralizes it, just as the natural sensor would.
Released from BCL2, the executioners spring into action, and the cancer cells finally die the death they should have died years ago.

The drug is so effective at unleashing apoptosis that doctors have to be careful about giving too much too fast — patients can develop tumor lysis syndrome, where so many cancer cells die at once that their contents flood the bloodstream and cause kidney failure and dangerous electrolyte shifts.
The drug works almost too well.
The frame to remember: apoptosis is a scheduled suicide that cancer cells have learned to refuse.
Many cancer therapies, including most of chemotherapy, ultimately work by raising the cellular stress to a level that finally re-triggers apoptosis.
We're not killing the cell ourselves so much as we're convincing it to commit the suicide it should have committed long ago.
